title: JavaScript: The Definitive Guide
author: David Flanagan (ingested) · Kata curriculum
description: Codecademy-style course derived from Flanagan's Definitive Guide, 7th ed. Each chapter mixes focused reading lessons with runnable exercises you can test.

lessons.title: What is a class?
lessons.body:
# What is a class?

Up to now we've worked with objects one at a time — each a unique bag of properties. But most programs deal with **groups of similar objects** that share structure and behavior.

A **class** is a template for creating those groups. Think of it this way: an object is "a specific dog named Rex", and a class is "what it means to be a dog."

Every class describes two things:

- **State** — the data each instance holds. A `Circle` gives each instance a `radius`. A `User` has a `name` and `email`.
- **Behavior** — methods that all instances share. `Circle` has an `area()` method that computes π × r².

Two objects that share the same behavior are **instances of the same class**.

```javascript
class Circle {
  constructor(radius) { this.radius = radius; }
  area() { return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2; }
}

const small = new Circle(1);
const large = new Circle(10);
small.area();  // 3.14159...
large.area();  // 314.159...
```

`small` and `large` hold different state (`radius: 1` vs `radius: 10`) but share the same `area()` method — defined **once** by the class and reused by every instance.

JavaScript classes have evolved over time. This chapter walks through three layers:

1. **Prototypes** — the mechanism classes are built on.
2. **Constructor functions** — classes before the `class` keyword.
3. **`class` syntax** — the modern form introduced in ES6.

Understanding all three makes you a much better JavaScript reader.

lessons.title: Prototypes and Object.create
Your first factory function
lessons.body:
# Build a range factory

Implement a `range(from, to)` factory function that returns an object with:

- A `from` property equal to the first argument
- A `to` property equal to the second argument
- Access to a shared `includes(x)` method and `toString()` method (already defined on `range.methods`)

All range instances must share the **same** method references — don't copy the methods onto each instance. Use `Object.create(range.methods)` to link each new object to the prototype.

## Requirements

- `range(1, 10).includes(5)` returns `true`
- `range(1, 10).includes(11)` returns `false`
- Two instances share the same `includes` function reference

## Hints

- `Object.create(proto)` returns a new object whose prototype is `proto`.
- `range.methods` is already defined for you below — don't redefine it, just link to it.

lessons.title: Constructor functions and `new`
Rewrite Range with a constructor
lessons.body:
# Rewrite Range with a constructor

Convert the factory from the previous exercise into a constructor function.

## Requirements

- Call the constructor `Range` (capital R)
- Assign `.from` and `.to` inside the constructor
- Attach `includes` and `toString` to `Range.prototype`
- `new Range(1, 10).includes(5)` returns `true`
- Two instances share the same method references

## Hint

Inside a function called with `new`, `this` refers to the fresh instance.

lessons.title: The `class` keyword
Range as a modern class
lessons.body:
# Range as a modern class

Rewrite `Range` using the `class` keyword.

## Requirements

- Use `class Range { ... }` syntax
- Include a `constructor(from, to)` that stores both as instance properties
- Define `includes(x)` and `toString()` inside the class body
- `new Range(1, 10).includes(5)` returns `true`
- Methods live on `Range.prototype`, not on each instance
lessons.starter:
// Write your class here.

module.exports = { Range };

lessons.title: Static methods, getters, private fields
Build a Complex number class
lessons.body:
# Build a Complex number class

Put static methods, getters, and private fields together. Implement a `Complex` class representing a complex number (`real + imaginary i`).

## Requirements

- `new Complex(real, imag)` creates an instance
- Private fields `#real` and `#imag` store the components (no direct access from outside)
- Getters `real` and `imag` return the respective components
- A `magnitude` getter returns `Math.sqrt(real² + imag²)`
- An `add(other)` method returns a **new** `Complex` with summed parts
- A static method `Complex.zero()` returns `new Complex(0, 0)`
- `toString()` renders `'a + bi'` — or `'a - bi'` when imag is negative

## Notes

- Don't mutate `this` in `add` — return a new `Complex`.
- For `toString`, use the imaginary part's sign to pick `+` or `-` and print the absolute value.

lessons.title: Inheritance with `extends` and `super`
Subclass: NumericRange
lessons.body:
# Subclass: NumericRange

The `Range` class from earlier works for any type that supports `<=`. Create a subclass `NumericRange` that adds numeric-only behavior.

## Requirements

`NumericRange`:

- Extends `Range`
- Has a `length` getter: `to - from + 1`
- Has a `toArray()` method returning `[from, from+1, ..., to]` (integers only)
- Overrides `toString()` to include the length: `'(1...5) [5 elements]'`

The parent `Range` class is already provided.

## Notes

- Don't redefine `includes`; it's inherited.
- Use `super.toString()` in your overridden `toString()`.
